 12→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer
 13→import kotlin.math.*
 14→import kotlin.time.Duration
 15→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 16→
 17→/**
 18→ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 19→ *
 20→ * The date components are: [years] ([DateTimeUnit.YEAR]), [months] ([DateTimeUnit.MONTH]), and [days] ([DateTimeUnit.DAY]).
 21→ *
 22→ * The time components are: [hours] ([DateTimeUnit.HOUR]), [minutes] ([DateTimeUnit.MINUTE]),
 23→ * [seconds] ([DateTimeUnit.SECOND]), and [nanoseconds] ([DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND]).
 24→ *
 25→ * The time components are not independent and are always normalized together.
 26→ * Likewise, months are normalized together with years.
 27→ * For example, there is no difference between `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, hours = 2, minutes = 63)` and
 28→ * `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, hours = 3, minutes = 3)`.
 29→ *
 30→ * All components can also be negative: for example, `DateTimePeriod(months = -5, days = 6, hours = -3)`.
 31→ * Whereas `months = 5` means "5 months after," `months = -5` means "5 months earlier."
 32→ *
 33→ * A constant time interval that consists of a single non-zero component (like "yearly" or "quarterly") should be
 34→ * represented by a [DateTimeUnit] directly instead of a [DateTimePeriod]:
 35→ * for example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one could use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
 36→ * This provides a wider variety of operations: for example, finding how many such intervals fit between two instants
 37→ * or dates or adding a multiple of such intervals at once.
 38→ *
 39→ * ### Interaction with other entities
 40→ *
 41→ * [DateTimePeriod] can be returned from [Instant.periodUntil], representing the difference between two instants.
 42→ * Conversely, there is an [Instant.plus] overload that accepts a [DateTimePeriod] and returns a new instant.
 43→ *
 44→ * [DatePeriod] is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal
 45→ * to zero.
 46→ *
 47→ * [DateTimePeriod] can be thought of as a combination of a [Duration] and a [DatePeriod], as it contains both the
 48→ * time components of [Duration] and the date components of [DatePeriod].
 49→ * [Duration.toDateTimePeriod] can be used to convert a [Duration] to the corresponding [DateTimePeriod].
 50→ *
 51→ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
 52→ *
 53→ * When a [DateTimePeriod] is constructed in any way, a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of [DateTimePeriod],
 54→ * will be returned if all time components happen to be zero.
 55→ *
 56→ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the constructor function with the same name.
 57→ * See sample 1.
 58→ *
 59→ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain a [DateTimePeriod] from and convert it to a string in the
 60→ * ISO 8601 extended format.
 61→ * See sample 2.
 62→ *
 63→ * `DateTimePeriod` can also be returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 64→ *
 65→ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [DateTimePeriod]:
 66→ * - [DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 format;
 67→ * - [DateTimePeriodComponentSerializer]  for an object with components.
 68→ *
 69→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.construction
 70→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
 71→ */
 72→@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 73→// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 74→public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 75→    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 76→
 77→    /**
 78→     * The number of calendar days. Can be negative.
 79→     *
 80→     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 81→     * Also, this field does not get normalized together with months, so values larger than 31 can be present.
 82→     *
 83→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 84→     */
 85→    public abstract val days: Int
 86→    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 87→
 88→    /**
 89→     * The number of whole years. Can be negative.
 90→     *
 91→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 92→     */
 93→    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 94→
 95→    /**
 96→     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 97→     *
 98→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
 99→     */
100→    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
101→
102→    /**
103→     * The number of whole hours in this period. Can be negative.
104→     *
105→     * This field does not get normalized together with days, so values larger than 23 or smaller than -23 can be present.
106→     *
107→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
108→     */
109→    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
110→
111→    /**
112→     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
113→     *
114→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
115→     */
116→    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
117→
118→    /**
119→     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
120→     *
121→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
122→     */
123→    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
124→
125→    /**
126→     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
127→     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
128→     *
129→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.valueNormalization
130→     */
131→    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
132→
133→    private fun allNonpositive() =
134→        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
135→
136→    /**
137→     * Converts this period to the ISO 8601 string representation for durations, for example, `P2M1DT3H`.
138→     *
139→     * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
140→     * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
141→     *
142→     * Examples of the output:
143→     * - `P2Y4M-1D`: two years, four months, minus one day;
144→     * - `-P2Y4M1D`: minus two years, minus four months, minus one day;
145→     * - `P1DT3H2M4.123456789S`: one day, three hours, two minutes, four seconds, 123456789 nanoseconds;
146→     * - `P1DT-3H-2M-4.123456789S`: one day, minus three hours, minus two minutes,
147→     *   minus four seconds, minus 123456789 nanoseconds;
148→     *
149→     * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a)
150→     *
151→     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse for the detailed description of the format.
152→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.toStringSample
153→     */
154→    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
155→        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
156→        append('P')
157→        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
158→        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
159→        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
160→        var t = "T"
161→        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
162→        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
163→        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
164→            append(t)
165→            append(when {
166→                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
167→                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
168→                else -> "0"
169→            })
170→            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
171→            append('S')
172→        }
173→
174→        if (length == 1) append("0D")
175→    }
176→
177→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
178→        if (this === other) return true
179→        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
180→
181→        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
182→        if (days != other.days) return false
183→        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
184→
185→        return true
186→    }
187→
188→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
189→        var result = totalMonths
190→        result = 31 * result + days
191→        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
192→        return result
193→    }
194→
195→    public companion object {
196→        /**
197→         * Parses a ISO 8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
198→         *
199→         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
200→         *
201→         * Note that the ISO 8601 duration is not the same as [Duration],
202→         * but instead includes the date components, like [DateTimePeriod] does.
203→         *
204→         * Examples of durations in the ISO 8601 format:
205→         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
206→         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
207→         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
208→         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
209→         *
210→         * The format is defined as follows:
211→         * - First, optionally, a `-` or `+`.
212→         *   If `-` is present, the whole period after the `-` is negated: `-P-2M1D` is the same as `P2M-1D`.
213→         * - Then, the letter `P`.
214→         * - Optionally, the number of years, followed by `Y`.
215→         * - Optionally, the number of months, followed by `M`.
216→         * - Optionally, the number of weeks, followed by `W`.
217→         * - Optionally, the number of days, followed by `D`.
218→         * - The string can end here if there are no more time components.
219→         *   If there are time components, the letter `T` is required.
220→         * - Optionally, the number of hours, followed by `H`.
221→         * - Optionally, the number of minutes, followed by `M`.
222→         * - Optionally, the number of seconds, followed by `S`.
223→         *   Seconds can optionally have a fractional part with up to nine digits.
224→         *   The fractional part is separated with a `.`.
225→         *
226→         * An explicit `+` or `-` sign can be prepended to any number.
227→         * `-` means that the number is negative, and `+` has no effect.
228→         *
229→         * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.5.2.2a) and 5.5.2.2b).
230→         * We combine the two formats into one by allowing the number of weeks to go after the number of months
231→         * and before the number of days.
232→         *
233→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
234→         * exceeded.
235→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.parsing
236→         */
237→        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
238→            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
239→                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
240→            val START = 0
241→            val AFTER_P = 1
242→            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
243→            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
244→            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
245→            val AFTER_DAY = 5
246→            val AFTER_T = 6
247→            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
248→            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
249→            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
250→
251→            var state = START
252→            // next unread character
253→            var i = 0
254→            var sign = 1
255→            var years = 0
256→            var months = 0
257→            var weeks = 0
258→            var days = 0
259→            var hours = 0
260→            var minutes = 0
261→            var seconds = 0
262→            var nanoseconds = 0
263→            while (true) {
264→                if (i >= text.length) {
265→                    if (state == START)
266→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
267→                    if (state == AFTER_T)
268→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
269→                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
270→                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
271→                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
272→                    }
273→                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
274→                }
275→                if (state == START) {
276→                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
277→                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
278→                    when (text[i]) {
279→                        '+', '-' -> {
280→                            if (text[i] == '-')
281→                                sign = -1
282→                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
283→                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
284→                            i += 2
285→                        }
286→                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
287→                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
288→                    }
289→                    state = AFTER_P
290→                    continue
291→                }
292→                var localSign = sign
293→                val iStart = i
294→                when (text[i]) {
295→                    '+', '-' -> {
296→                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
297→                        i += 1
298→                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
299→                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
300→                    }
301→                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
302→                    'T' -> {
303→                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
304→                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
305→                        state = AFTER_T
306→                        i += 1
307→                        continue
308→                    }
309→                }
310→                var number = 0L
311→                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
312→                    try {
313→                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
314→                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
315→                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
316→                    }
317→                    i += 1
318→                }
319→                number *= localSign
320→                if (i == text.length)
321→                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
322→                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
323→                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
324→                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
325→                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
326→                    return toInt()
327→                }
328→                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
329→                    'Y' -> {
330→                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
331→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
332→                        state = AFTER_YEAR
333→                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
334→                    }
335→                    'M' -> {
336→                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
337→                            // Minutes
338→                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
339→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
340→                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
341→                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
342→                        } else {
343→                            // Months
344→                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
345→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
346→                            state = AFTER_MONTH
347→                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
348→                        }
349→                    }
350→                    'W' -> {
351→                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
352→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
353→                        state = AFTER_WEEK
354→                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
355→                    }
356→                    'D' -> {
357→                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
358→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
359→                        state = AFTER_DAY
360→                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
361→                    }
362→                    'H' -> {
363→                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
364→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
365→                        state = AFTER_HOUR
366→                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
367→                    }
368→                    'S' -> {
369→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
370→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
371→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
372→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
373→                    }
374→                    '.', ',' -> {
375→                        i += 1
376→                        if (i >= text.length)
377→                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
378→                        val iStartFraction = i
379→                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')

381→                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
382→                        if (fractionLength > 9)
383→                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
384→                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)

386→                        if (text[i] != 'S')
387→                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
388→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
389→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)

391→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
392→                    }
393→                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
394→                }

407→/**
408→ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores the date components and has all time components equal to zero.
409→ *
410→ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
411→ * the time components are zero.
412→ *
413→ * ```
414→ * DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y3D") as DatePeriod // 1 year and 3 days
415→ * ```

417→ * Additionally, [DatePeriod] has its own constructor, the [parse] function that will fail if any of the time components
418→ * are not zero, and [DatePeriodIso8601Serializer] and [DatePeriodComponentSerializer], mirroring those of
419→ * [DateTimePeriod].
420→ *
421→ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations
422→ * on [LocalDates][LocalDate], but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
423→ *
424→ * On the JVM, there are `DatePeriod.toJavaPeriod()` and `java.time.Period.toKotlinDatePeriod()`
425→ * extension functions to convert between `kotlinx.datetime` and `java.time` objects used for the same purpose.
426→ *
427→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.simpleParsingAndFormatting
428→ */
429→@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
430→public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
431→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
432→    override val days: Int,
433→) : DateTimePeriod() {
434→    /**
435→     * Constructs a new [DatePeriod].
436→     *
437→     * It is recommended to always name the arguments explicitly when constructing this manually,
438→     * like `DatePeriod(years = 1, months = 12, days = 16)`.
439→     *
440→     * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
441→     * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
442→     *
443→     * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
444→     * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DateBased] instead.
445→     * For example, instead of `DatePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
446→     *
447→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
448→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.construction
449→     */
450→    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
451→    // avoiding excessive computations
452→    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
453→    override val hours: Int get() = 0
454→
455→    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
456→    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
457→
458→    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
459→    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
460→
461→    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
462→    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
463→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
464→
465→    public companion object {
466→        /**
467→         * Parses the ISO 8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod], for example, `P1Y2M30D`.
468→         *
469→         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
470→         * zero.
471→         *
472→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
473→         * or any time components are not zero.
474→         *
475→         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
476→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DatePeriodSamples.parsing
477→         */
478→        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
479→            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
480→                is DatePeriod -> period
481→                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
482→            }
483→    }
484→}
485→
486→/**
487→ * @suppress
488→ */
489→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
490→public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
491→
492→private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
493→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
494→    override val days: Int,
495→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
496→) : DateTimePeriod()
497→
498→// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
499→private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
500→    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
501→        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
502→        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
503→    }
504→
505→private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
506→    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
507→    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
508→    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
509→    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
510→    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
511→    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
512→    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
513→    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
514→    return try {
515→        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
516→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
517→        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
518→    }
519→}
520→
521→internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
522→    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
523→        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
524→    else
525→        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
526→
527→/**
528→ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
529→ *
530→ * It is recommended to always name the arguments explicitly when constructing this manually,
531→ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12, days = 16)`.
532→ *
533→ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
534→ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24, days = 41)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2, days = 41)`.
535→ *
536→ * If only a single component is set and is always non-zero and is semantically a fixed time interval
537→ * (like "yearly" or "quarterly"), please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit] instead.
538→ * For example, instead of `DateTimePeriod(months = 6)`, one can use `DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 6`.
539→ *
540→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
541→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
542→ * overflows a [Long].
543→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.constructorFunction
544→ */
545→public fun DateTimePeriod(
546→    years: Int = 0,
547→    months: Int = 0,
548→    days: Int = 0,
549→    hours: Int = 0,
550→    minutes: Int = 0,
551→    seconds: Int = 0,
552→    nanoseconds: Long = 0
553→): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
554→    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
555→
556→/**
557→ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
558→ *
559→ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
560→ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
561→ *
562→ * **Pitfall**: a [DateTimePeriod] obtained this way will always have zero date components.
563→ * The reason is that even a [Duration] obtained via [Duration.Companion.days] just means a multiple of 24 hours,
564→ * whereas in `kotlinx-datetime`, a day is a calendar day, which can be different from 24 hours.
565→ * See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
566→ *
567→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.DateTimePeriodSamples.durationToDateTimePeriod
568→ */
569→// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
570→public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
571→
572→/**
573→ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
574→ *
575→ * **Pitfall**: given three instants, adding together the periods between the first and the second and between the
576→ * second and the third *does not* necessarily equal the period between the first and the third.
577→ *
578→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
579→ */
580→public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
581→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
582→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
583→    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
584→)
585→
586→/**
587→ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
588→ *
589→ * **Pitfall**: given three dates, adding together the periods between the first and the second and between the
590→ * second and the third *does not* necessarily equal the period between the first and the third.
591→ *
592→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
593→ */
594→public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
595→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
596→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
597→)

 12→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.LocalDateIso8601Serializer

 14→import java.time.DateTimeException

 18→
 19→@Serializable(with = LocalDateIso8601Serializer::class)
 20→public actual class LocalDate internal constructor(internal val value: jtLocalDate) : Comparable<LocalDate> {
 21→    public actual companion object {
 22→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>): LocalDate =
 23→            if (format === Formats.ISO) {
 24→                try {
 25→                    jtLocalDate.parse(input).let(::LocalDate)
 26→                } catch (e: DateTimeParseException) {
 27→                    throw DateTimeFormatException(e)
 28→                }
 29→            } else {
 30→                format.parse(input)
 31→            }
 32→
 33→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
 34→        public fun parse(isoString: String): LocalDate = parse(input = isoString)
 35→
 36→        internal actual val MIN: LocalDate = LocalDate(jtLocalDate.MIN)
 37→        internal actual val MAX: LocalDate = LocalDate(jtLocalDate.MAX)
 38→
 39→        public actual fun fromEpochDays(epochDays: Int): LocalDate =
 40→            LocalDate(jtLocalDate.ofEpochDay(epochDays.toLong()))
 41→
 42→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 43→        public actual fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> =
 44→            LocalDateFormat.build(block)
 45→    }
 46→
 47→    public actual object Formats {
 48→        public actual val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> get() = ISO_DATE
 49→
 50→        public actual val ISO_BASIC: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> = ISO_DATE_BASIC
 51→    }
 52→
 53→    public actual constructor(year: Int, monthNumber: Int, dayOfMonth: Int) :
 54→            this(try {
 55→                jtLocalDate.of(year, monthNumber, dayOfMonth)
 56→            } catch (e: DateTimeException) {
 57→                throw IllegalArgumentException(e)
 58→            })
 59→
 60→    public actual constructor(year: Int, month: Month, dayOfMonth: Int) : this(year, month.number, dayOfMonth)
 61→
 62→    public actual val year: Int get() = value.year
 63→    public actual val monthNumber: Int get() = value.monthValue
 64→    public actual val month: Month get() = value.month
 65→    public actual val dayOfMonth: Int get() = value.dayOfMonth
 66→    public actual val dayOfWeek: DayOfWeek get() = value.dayOfWeek
 67→    public actual val dayOfYear: Int get() = value.dayOfYear
 68→
 69→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 70→            (this === other) || (other is LocalDate && this.value == other.value)
 71→
 72→    override fun hashCode(): Int = value.hashCode()
 73→
 74→    actual override fun toString(): String = value.toString()
 75→
 76→    actual override fun compareTo(other: LocalDate): Int = this.value.compareTo(other.value)
 77→
 78→    public actual fun toEpochDays(): Int = value.toEpochDay().clampToInt()
 79→}
 80→
 81→@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit)"))
 82→public actual fun LocalDate.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate =
 83→        plus(1L, unit)
 84→
 85→public actual fun LocalDate.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate =
 86→        plus(value.toLong(), unit)
 87→
 88→public actual fun LocalDate.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate =
 89→        plus(-value.toLong(), unit)
 90→
 91→public actual fun LocalDate.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate =
 92→        try {
 93→            when (unit) {
 94→                is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> {
 95→                    val addDays: Long = safeMultiply(value, unit.days.toLong())
 96→                    ofEpochDayChecked(safeAdd(this.value.toEpochDay(), addDays))
 97→                }
 98→                is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased ->
 99→                    this.value.plusMonths(safeMultiply(value, unit.months.toLong()))
100→            }.let(::LocalDate)
101→        } catch (e: Exception) {
102→            if (e !is DateTimeException && e !is ArithmeticException) throw e
103→            throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The result of adding $value of $unit to $this is out of LocalDate range.", e)
104→        }
105→
106→private val minEpochDay = java.time.LocalDate.MIN.toEpochDay()
107→private val maxEpochDay = java.time.LocalDate.MAX.toEpochDay()
108→private fun ofEpochDayChecked(epochDay: Long): java.time.LocalDate {
109→    // LocalDate.ofEpochDay doesn't actually check that the argument doesn't overflow year calculation
110→    if (epochDay !in minEpochDay..maxEpochDay)
111→        throw DateTimeException("The resulting day $epochDay is out of supported LocalDate range.")
112→    return java.time.LocalDate.ofEpochDay(epochDay)
113→}
114→
115→public actual operator fun LocalDate.plus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate = try {
116→    with(period) {
117→        return@with value
118→                .run { if (totalMonths != 0) plusMonths(totalMonths.toLong()) else this }
119→                .run { if (days != 0) plusDays(days.toLong()) else this }
120→
121→    }.let(::LocalDate)
122→} catch (e: DateTimeException) {
123→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The result of adding $value to $this is out of LocalDate range.")
124→}
125→
126→
127→public actual fun LocalDate.periodUntil(other: LocalDate): DatePeriod {
128→    var startD = this.value
129→    val endD = other.value
130→    val months = startD.until(endD, ChronoUnit.MONTHS); startD = startD.plusMonths(months)
131→    val days = startD.until(endD, ChronoUnit.DAYS)
132→
133→    if (months > Int.MAX_VALUE || months < Int.MIN_VALUE) {
134→        throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The number of months between $this and $other does not fit in an Int")
135→    }
136→    return DatePeriod(totalMonths = months.toInt(), days.toInt())
137→}
138→
139→public actual fun LocalDate.until(other: LocalDate, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): Int = when(unit) {
140→    is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased -> (this.value.until(other.value, ChronoUnit.MONTHS) / unit.months).clampToInt()
141→    is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> (this.value.until(other.value, ChronoUnit.DAYS) / unit.days).clampToInt()
142→}
143→
144→public actual fun LocalDate.daysUntil(other: LocalDate): Int =
145→        this.value.until(other.value, ChronoUnit.DAYS).clampToInt()
146→
147→public actual fun LocalDate.monthsUntil(other: LocalDate): Int =
148→        this.value.until(other.value, ChronoUnit.MONTHS).clampToInt()
149→
150→public actual fun LocalDate.yearsUntil(other: LocalDate): Int =
151→        this.value.until(other.value, ChronoUnit.YEARS).clampToInt()

107→                .run { if (days != 0) jsTry { plusDays(days) } else this }

117→public actual fun LocalDate.periodUntil(other: LocalDate): DatePeriod {
118→    var startD = this.value
119→    val endD = other.value

390→    AbstractDateTimeFormat<LocalDate, IncompleteLocalDate>() {

392→        IncompleteLocalDate().apply { populateFrom(value) }

395→
396→    override val emptyIntermediate get() = emptyIncompleteLocalDate
397→
398→    companion object {

400→            val builder = Builder(AppendableFormatStructure())
401→            builder.block()
402→            return LocalDateFormat(builder.build())
403→        }
404→    }
405→
406→    internal class Builder(override val actualBuilder: AppendableFormatStructure<DateFieldContainer>) :
407→        AbstractDateTimeFormatBuilder<DateFieldContainer, Builder>, AbstractWithDateBuilder {
408→
409→        override fun addFormatStructureForDate(structure: FormatStructure<DateFieldContainer>) =
410→            actualBuilder.add(structure)
411→

413→    }

 12→/**
 13→ * A directive that specifies a way to parse and format the [field].
 14→ */
 15→internal interface FieldFormatDirective<in Target> {
 16→    /**
 17→     * The field parsed and formatted by this directive.
 18→     */
 19→    val field: FieldSpec<Target, *>
 20→
 21→    /**
 22→     * The formatter operation that formats the field.
 23→     */
 24→    fun formatter(): FormatterStructure<Target>
 25→
 26→    /**
 27→     * The parser structure that parses the field.
 28→     */
 29→    fun parser(): ParserStructure<Target>
 30→
 31→    /**
 32→     * The string with the code that, when evaluated in the builder context, appends the directive.
 33→     */
 34→    val builderRepresentation: String
 35→}
 36→
 37→/**
 38→ * A directive for a decimal format of an integer field that is known to be unsigned.
 39→ * The field is formatted with the field padded to [minDigits] with zeroes,
 40→ * and the parser expects the field to be at least [minDigits] digits long.
 41→ */
 42→internal abstract class UnsignedIntFieldFormatDirective<in Target>(
 43→    final override val field: UnsignedFieldSpec<Target>,
 44→    private val minDigits: Int,
 45→    private val spacePadding: Int?,
 46→) : FieldFormatDirective<Target> {
 47→
 48→    private val maxDigits: Int = field.maxDigits
 49→
 50→    init {
 51→        require(minDigits >= 0) {
 52→            "The minimum number of digits ($minDigits) is negative"
 53→        }
 54→        require(maxDigits >= minDigits) {
 55→            "The maximum number of digits ($maxDigits) is less than the minimum number of digits ($minDigits)"
 56→        }
 57→        if (spacePadding != null) {
 58→            require(spacePadding > minDigits) {
 59→                "The space padding ($spacePadding) should be more than the minimum number of digits ($minDigits)"
 60→            }
 61→        }
 62→    }
 63→
 64→    override fun formatter(): FormatterStructure<Target> {
 65→        val formatter = UnsignedIntFormatterStructure(
 66→            number = field.accessor::getterNotNull,
 67→            zeroPadding = minDigits,
 68→        )
 69→        return if (spacePadding != null) SpacePaddedFormatter(formatter, spacePadding) else formatter
 70→    }
 71→
 72→    override fun parser(): ParserStructure<Target> =
 73→        spaceAndZeroPaddedUnsignedInt(minDigits, maxDigits, spacePadding, field.accessor, field.name)
 74→}
 75→
 76→/**
 77→ * A directive for a string-based format of an integer field that is known to be unsigned.
 78→ */
 79→internal abstract class NamedUnsignedIntFieldFormatDirective<in Target>(
 80→    final override val field: UnsignedFieldSpec<Target>,
 81→    private val values: List<String>,
 82→    private val name: String,
 83→) : FieldFormatDirective<Target> {
 84→
 85→    init {
 86→        require(values.size == field.maxValue - field.minValue + 1) {
 87→            "The number of values (${values.size}) in $values does not match the range of the field (${field.maxValue - field.minValue + 1})"
 88→        }
 89→    }
 90→
 91→    private fun getStringValue(target: Target): String = field.accessor.getterNotNull(target).let {
 92→        values.getOrNull(it-field.minValue)
 93→            ?: "The value $it of ${field.name} does not have a corresponding string representation"
 94→    }
 95→
 96→    private inner class AssignableString: AssignableField<Target, String> {
 97→        override fun trySetWithoutReassigning(container: Target, newValue: String): String? =
 98→            field.accessor.trySetWithoutReassigning(container, values.indexOf(newValue) + field.minValue)?.let {
 99→                values[it - field.minValue]
100→            }
101→
102→        override val name: String get() = this@NamedUnsignedIntFieldFormatDirective.name
103→    }
104→
105→    override fun formatter(): FormatterStructure<Target> =
106→        StringFormatterStructure(::getStringValue)
107→
108→    override fun parser(): ParserStructure<Target> =
109→        ParserStructure(
110→            listOf(
111→                StringSetParserOperation(values, AssignableString(), "one of $values for $name")
112→            ), emptyList()
113→        )
114→}

137→    }

31→        require(monthNumber in 1..12) { "Invalid date: month must be a number between 1 and 12, got $monthNumber" }
32→        require(dayOfMonth in 1..31) { "Invalid date: day of month must be a number between 1 and 31, got $dayOfMonth" }
33→        if (dayOfMonth > 28 && dayOfMonth > monthNumber.monthLength(isLeapYear(year))) {
34→            if (dayOfMonth == 29) {

 9→package kotlinx.datetime

14→import kotlinx.serialization.*
15→
16→@Serializable(with = LocalDateTimeIso8601Serializer::class)
17→public actual class LocalDateTime
18→public actual constructor(public actual val date: LocalDate, public actual val time: LocalTime) : Comparable<LocalDateTime> {
19→    public actual companion object {
20→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime>): LocalDateTime =
21→            format.parse(input)
22→
23→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
24→        public fun parse(isoString: String): LocalDateTime = parse(input = isoString)
25→
26→        internal actual val MIN: LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime(LocalDate.MIN, LocalTime.MIN)
27→        internal actual val MAX: LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime(LocalDate.MAX, LocalTime.MAX)
28→
29→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
30→        public actual fun Format(builder: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTime.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime> =
31→            LocalDateTimeFormat.build(builder)
32→    }
33→
34→    public actual object Formats {
35→        public actual val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime> = ISO_DATETIME
36→    }
37→
38→    public actual constructor(year: Int, monthNumber: Int, dayOfMonth: Int, hour: Int, minute: Int, second: Int, nanosecond: Int) :
39→        this(LocalDate(year, monthNumber, dayOfMonth), LocalTime.of(hour, minute, second, nanosecond))
40→
41→    public actual constructor(year: Int, month: Month, dayOfMonth: Int, hour: Int, minute: Int, second: Int, nanosecond: Int) :
42→        this(LocalDate(year, month, dayOfMonth), LocalTime.of(hour, minute, second, nanosecond))
43→
44→    public actual val year: Int get() = date.year
45→    public actual val monthNumber: Int get() = date.monthNumber
46→    public actual val month: Month get() = date.month
47→    public actual val dayOfMonth: Int get() = date.dayOfMonth
48→    public actual val dayOfWeek: DayOfWeek get() = date.dayOfWeek
49→    public actual val dayOfYear: Int get() = date.dayOfYear
50→    public actual val hour: Int get() = time.hour
51→    public actual val minute: Int get() = time.minute
52→    public actual val second: Int get() = time.second
53→    public actual val nanosecond: Int get() = time.nanosecond
54→
55→    // Several times faster than using `compareBy`
56→    actual override fun compareTo(other: LocalDateTime): Int {
57→        val d = date.compareTo(other.date)
58→        if (d != 0) {
59→            return d
60→        }
61→        return time.compareTo(other.time)
62→    }
63→
64→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
65→        this === other || (other is LocalDateTime && compareTo(other) == 0)
66→
67→    // org.threeten.bp.LocalDateTime#hashCode
68→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
69→        return date.hashCode() xor time.hashCode()
70→    }
71→
72→    // org.threeten.bp.LocalDateTime#toString
73→    actual override fun toString(): String = format(ISO_DATETIME_OPTIONAL_SECONDS_TRAILING_ZEROS)
74→
75→    // org.threeten.bp.chrono.ChronoLocalDateTime#toEpochSecond
76→    internal fun toEpochSecond(offset: UtcOffset): Long {
77→        val epochDay = date.toEpochDays().toLong()
78→        var secs: Long = epochDay * 86400 + time.toSecondOfDay()
79→        secs -= offset.totalSeconds
80→        return secs

package kotlinx.datetime.test

import kotlinx.datetime.*
import kotlin.test.*

/**
 * Tests for bug fixes from https://gist.github.com/ilma4/a5ad5b5c284969e24f4e2149a2f8b273
 */
class BugFixesTest {

    @Test
    fun testDatePeriodParseRejectsEmptyP() {
        // "P" without any components should fail according to ISO 8601
        assertFailsWith<DateTimeFormatException> {
            DateTimePeriod.parse("P")
        }
        assertFailsWith<DateTimeFormatException> {
            DatePeriod.parse("P")
        }
    }

    @Test
    fun testLocalDateParseWithManyLeadingZeroes() {
        // Test parsing dates with many leading zeroes in the year
        val date1 = LocalDate.parse("+00000000000002022-07-16")
        assertEquals(2022, date1.year)
        assertEquals(7, date1.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, date1.dayOfMonth)

        val date2 = LocalDate.Formats.ISO.parse("+00000000000002022-07-16")
        assertEquals(2022, date2.year)
        assertEquals(7, date2.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, date2.dayOfMonth)

        // Test with negative year
        val date3 = LocalDate.parse("-00000000000002022-07-16")
        assertEquals(-2022, date3.year)
        assertEquals(7, date3.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, date3.dayOfMonth)

        // Test regular format still works
        val date4 = LocalDate.parse("2022-07-16")
        assertEquals(2022, date4.year)
        assertEquals(7, date4.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, date4.dayOfMonth)

        // Test with + sign and minimum extra digits
        val date5 = LocalDate.parse("+02022-07-16")
        assertEquals(2022, date5.year)
        assertEquals(7, date5.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, date5.dayOfMonth)
    }

    @Test
    fun testLocalDateTimeParseWithManyLeadingZeroes() {
        // Test parsing datetimes with many leading zeroes in the year
        val dateTime1 = LocalDateTime.parse("+00000000000002022-07-16T10:30:00")
        assertEquals(2022, dateTime1.year)
        assertEquals(7, dateTime1.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, dateTime1.dayOfMonth)
        assertEquals(10, dateTime1.hour)
        assertEquals(30, dateTime1.minute)
        assertEquals(0, dateTime1.second)

        val dateTime2 = LocalDateTime.Formats.ISO.parse("+00000000000002022-07-16T10:30:00")
        assertEquals(2022, dateTime2.year)
        assertEquals(7, dateTime2.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, dateTime2.dayOfMonth)
        assertEquals(10, dateTime2.hour)
        assertEquals(30, dateTime2.minute)
        assertEquals(0, dateTime2.second)

        // Test with negative year
        val dateTime3 = LocalDateTime.parse("-00000000000002022-07-16T10:30:00")
        assertEquals(-2022, dateTime3.year)
        assertEquals(7, dateTime3.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, dateTime3.dayOfMonth)
        assertEquals(10, dateTime3.hour)
        assertEquals(30, dateTime3.minute)
        assertEquals(0, dateTime3.second)

        // Test regular format still works
        val dateTime4 = LocalDateTime.parse("2022-07-16T10:30:00")
        assertEquals(2022, dateTime4.year)
        assertEquals(7, dateTime4.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, dateTime4.dayOfMonth)
        assertEquals(10, dateTime4.hour)
        assertEquals(30, dateTime4.minute)
        assertEquals(0, dateTime4.second)

        // Test with lowercase 't' separator (alternative parsing)
        val dateTime5 = LocalDateTime.parse("2022-07-16t10:30:00")
        assertEquals(2022, dateTime5.year)
        assertEquals(7, dateTime5.monthNumber)
        assertEquals(16, dateTime5.dayOfMonth)
        assertEquals(10, dateTime5.hour)
        assertEquals(30, dateTime5.minute)
        assertEquals(0, dateTime5.second)
    }
}
"}
